language: Spanish (PCIC baseline: northern-central Peninsular; variety_check cards adjust to the family's country)
source: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes — Niveles de referencia para el español (2006), A2 inventories

cards.title: What you've done (pretérito perfecto)
cards.goal: Can talk about recent events and life experiences.
grammar: he/has/ha + participio: he comido, he estado
hoy, esta semana, ya / todavía no, alguna vez
cards.evidence: Six true sentences about today/this week plus ask and answer two ¿alguna vez? experience questions.
cards.note: Much of Latin America prefers the indefinido here — if the family is LatAm, compress this card and expand ES-A2-02.

cards.title: What happened (indefinido)
grammar: regular indefinido + heavy irregulars: fui, estuve, hice, tuve, dije, vino
cards.evidence: Tell yesterday in six sentences using at least four indefinido forms correctly.

cards.title: Telling a story
cards.goal: Can tell a short connected story about a past experience.
discourse: sequencers: primero, luego, después, al final
cards.evidence: A 60-90 second true story with three sequencers; pass = the listener can retell the events in order.
skin_examples: how she met the boyfriend — guaranteed family-audience hit

cards.title: How things were (imperfecto)
cards.goal: Can describe past states, backgrounds and habits.
cards.evidence: Five sentences contrasting then vs now, imperfecto vs present correctly split.
skin_examples: her childhood in Flanders vs now
cards.note: Descriptive uses only; imperfecto/indefinido narrative weaving is B1.

cards.title: Plans & arrangements (quedar)
cards.goal: Can describe plans and make arrangements to meet.
grammar: quedar: ¿Quedamos el sábado? Hemos quedado a las dos
cards.evidence: Describe next week (four planned actions) and complete a quedar-exchange fixing day, time and place.

cards.title: Suggesting & inviting
cards.goal: Can make and respond to suggestions and invitations.
cards.evidence: Consensus roleplay: agree on a plan making two suggestions and politely deflecting one with an es-que excuse.

cards.title: Lo, la, le (object pronouns)
cards.goal: Can use direct and indirect object pronouns with common verbs.
grammar: placement: before conjugated verb / attached to infinitive — ¿Me lo pasas? Voy a llamarla
cards.evidence: Answer ten rapid questions whose natural answers need a pronoun (¿Has visto a tu suegra? — Sí, la he visto), 7/10 correct.

cards.title: Have to, must, can
cards.goal: Can express obligation, necessity and permission.
cards.evidence: Explain the rules of something familiar: two tener que, two hay que, two (no) se puede.

cards.title: Comparing
cards.evidence: Rank three items with two comparatives and one superlative, agreement correct.
skin_examples: Belgian vs Spanish food — diplomatic minefield, excellent practice

cards.title: Asking & offering
cards.goal: Can make polite requests and offers and respond to them.
functions: pedir y ofrecer ayuda y objetos
grammar: register: tú as family default in Spain; usted/ustedes weighting is a variety+family question
cards.evidence: Three-scene roleplay (ask for help, offer help, borrow something), natural responses in each.

cards.title: Reacting like a person, not a textbook
cards.goal: Can react in conversation with natural spoken markers.
functions: interactuar informalmente, expresar interés y sorpresa
discourse: Spain set: ¡Qué bien!, ¡Qué pena!, ¿En serio?, ¡No me digas!, vale, venga, claro, bueno…, ¡qué va!
LatAm swaps by country — confirm family origin, then fix the list
cards.evidence: During a five-minute chat, react appropriately eight times using at least four different markers.
skin_examples: reacting to the boyfriend's side of a real family story
cards.note: Five of these make her sound twice as fluent to family ears at zero grammatical cost.

cards.title: Simple opinions
cards.goal: Can give a simple opinion and agree/disagree gently.
exponents: creo que… / pienso que… / para mí…
cards.evidence: React to six statements: three agreements, two soft disagreements, one no-sé — each with one reason sentence.
skin_examples: food, films, Belgium vs Spain — low-stakes topics only, by design

cards.title: Describing people & places
cards.goal: Can give a short connected description of a person or place.
grammar: place frames: está en…, tiene…, es famoso por…
cards.evidence: A six-sentence spoken portrait of one family member (appearance + personality + one habit), recognisable to the boyfriend.

cards.title: WhatsApp Spanish
cards.goal: Can hold a short written/voice exchange in the family's everyday register.
discourse: closers: besos, un beso, abrazos — culturally standard, not romantic; state this explicitly
lexis: real family messages (with permission) as authentic texts
cards.evidence: A three-turn WhatsApp exchange (text or voice) in Spanish only; pass = the exchange completes naturally.

cards.title: When it's too fast (repair kit)
cards.goal: Can keep a conversation alive when he/she doesn't understand, without switching to Dutch/English or freezing.
cards.evidence: Deliberate-obstacle task: a 90-second slightly-too-fast story; reach full understanding with 3+ different repair moves, summarise back in Dutch, give a one-sentence Spanish reaction.
skin_examples: any real family story — the hardest real-world moment, rehearsed safely
cards.note: Family Spanish is fast, overlapping and loud. Pull this card forward as soon as A1 Strands A-B exist.
